rules.message: Async Express/router handler with no surrounding try/catch, `next(err)`, or `.catch()` — on Express 4, an unhandled rejection in an async handler crashes or hangs the process; Express 5 forwards async rejections to error-handling middleware automatically, so there it's a style/consistency concern rather than a crash bug. Wrap in try/catch and call `next(err)`, or attach `.catch(next)`. Suppress a vetted case with `// route-catch-ok`.

Synchronous fs/child_process call (`readFileSync`, `execSync`, etc.) alongside request-handler context (`req`/`res`/`ctx`) — a blocking call on the request path stalls Node's event loop for every concurrent request. Use the async `fs.promises`/`child_process` APIs, or move CPU-heavy work to a worker thread. Suppress a vetted case with `// sync-io-ok`.

`.map(async ...)` used without wrapping the result in `Promise.all`/`Promise.allSettled` — `.map` returns pending Promises, so a rejection becomes unhandled and any ordering/completion guarantee is lost. Wrap the call in `Promise.all(...)` (or `Promise.allSettled(...)` for expected partial failures). Suppress a vetted case with `// map-async-ok`.

`process.env.X!` non-null assertion — bypasses the null check and defers the crash from startup to first use, wherever that happens to be. Validate required env vars once at startup (a config module that throws if any are missing) and reference the validated value elsewhere. Suppress a vetted case with `// env-assert-ok`.

Debug flag or disabled TLS verification (`debug: true`, `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`, `rejectUnauthorized: false`) committed to source — ships a dev backdoor or TLS bypass to production if deployed unchanged. Remove the flag or gate it behind an environment check (e.g. `NODE_ENV !== 'production'`). Suppress a vetted line with `// debug-ok`.

`Promise.all(...)` used with DB write calls on a DB-client-shaped receiver (`prisma`/`db`/`tx`/`client`/`repo`) — non-atomic: some writes land and others don't when one rejects, with no rollback for what committed. Wrap the writes in a transaction, or use `Promise.allSettled(...)` with compensation logic. Suppress a vetted case with `// promise-all-ok`.

`JSON.parse(...)` called directly on apparent external input (`req`/`body`/`params`/`query`/`payload`/`event`) without a surrounding `try` — malformed input throws, turning a bad request into a 500 or a process crash. Wrap the parse in try/catch and return a proper 4xx on failure. Suppress a vetted case with `// json-parse-ok`.

Outbound HTTP call (`fetch`/`axios`/`got`) in server/worker code with no timeout or `AbortController` visible in the same function — `fetch` has no default timeout, so a hung upstream hangs the request and can exhaust the connection pool. Pass an explicit timeout (e.g. `AbortSignal.timeout(...)`) or wire up an `AbortController`. Suppress a vetted case with `// fetch-timeout-ok`.

`process.exit(...)` called inside a function — skips pending I/O flushes and cleanup handlers, killing the entire process, not just the current request. Throw (or reject) instead and let a top-level handler decide; legitimate only in a CLI entrypoint or a signal-handling module. Suppress a vetted case with `// process-exit-ok`.

Body parser (`express.json`/`urlencoded`, `bodyParser.json`/`urlencoded`) configured without an explicit `limit` — the code relies on the implicit 100kb default, which is too small for some legitimate endpoints and simply unexamined for the rest. Set an explicit `limit` (e.g. `{ limit: '1mb' }`) sized to the endpoint's needs. Suppress a vetted case with `// body-limit-ok`.

`console.*` call in backend-path source (`api/`, `server/`, `backend/`, `be/`, `routes/`, `controllers/`, `services/`) — console writes are synchronous and unstructured. Use the project's structured logger instead so output is queryable, non-blocking, and carries request context. Suppress a vetted case with `// console-ok`.

`setInterval(...)` with no matching `clearInterval(...)` anywhere in this file — a leaked timer keeps the process (or page) alive and re-fires forever, since nothing cancels it. Store the return value in a handle and call `clearInterval(handle)` during teardown/cleanup. Suppress a vetted case with `// interval-ok`.

`process.env.X` accessed outside a config module — scatters env parsing/validation across the codebase, making it hard to know what env vars the app depends on or validate them at startup. Centralize env reads in one config module and import the validated value elsewhere. Suppress a vetted case with `// env-access-ok`.
